perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” shall include all technical and non-technical information provided by either party to th e other, including but not limited to materials generated by Khronos and by Members on behalf of Khronos, and not specifically designated as non- Confidential by the providing party, incl uding all versions and revisions of draft specifications and any passwords and minutes provided to either Participant or Khronos under this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” shall include only technical information provided by either party to the other, specifically designated as Confidential by the providing party, including the final version of specifications. No passwords or minutes provided to either Participant or Khronos under this agreement are Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition significantly narrows the scope of "Confidential Information," excluding non-technical information, draft specifications, passwords, and minutes, and requiring explicit designation of confidentiality. This conflicts with the standard understanding and legal interpretation of confidential information in NDAs, which often covers a broader range of materials.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA defines trade secrets (a subset of confidential information) broadly, including technical and non-technical information that derives independent economic value. The modified contract language, by restricting confidential information to only 'technical information' and requiring it to be 'specifically designated as Confidential,' could exclude information that would otherwise be protected as a trade secret under CUTSA, such as marketing plans or customer lists.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language contradicts the spirit and scope of trade secret protection under CUTSA, as explained in the Law Explanation. The original text covers technical and non-technical information, while the changed text limits it to only technical information specifically designated as confidential, potentially excluding items that would otherwise be protected. Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.1, defines misappropriation broadly, and the modified contract language could allow for the legal acquisition or disclosure of information that should otherwise be considered a trade secret, undermining its protection.

perturbation.original_text: “Effective Date” means the later of the last date of si gnature on the signature page below.
perturbation.changed_text: “Effective Date” means the date Khronos internally approves this agreement, regardless of the signature dates.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the definition of "Effective Date" to when Khronos approves it internally, rather than the signature date, creates a conflict. It introduces uncertainty about when the agreement legally binds the Participant, potentially invalidating the agreement due to lack of mutual agreement on a key term.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Basic Principles of Contract Law - Offer and Acceptance
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1565 requires mutual consent for a contract to be valid. Defining the effective date solely by Khronos's internal approval, irrespective of the Participant's signature date, undermines the principle of mutual consent and creates a unilateral condition, making the agreement potentially unenforceable because the participant does not control the effective date.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1565. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1. NATURE OF A CONTRACT [1549 - 1615] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Consent [1565 - 1590] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 1565. The consent of the parties to a contract must be: 1. Free; 2. Mutual; and, 3. Communicated by each to the other. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the "Effective Date" definition from the signature date to Khronos's internal approval date. This contradicts California Civil Code § 1565, as it undermines the requirement of mutual consent. The original text ensures both parties agree by signing, while the changed text makes the agreement effective solely based on Khronos's decision, potentially without the Participant's knowledge or agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "The consent of the parties to a contract must be: 1. Free; 2. Mutual; and, 3. Communicated by each to the other." The changed text violates the 'Mutual' consent requirement, making the contract potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Both parties will maintain all Confidential Information in confidence with at least the same degree of care that it uses to protect its ow n confidential and propri etary information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care unde r the circumstances and will not disclose the Confidential Information except as necessary for its authorized participants to evaluate and comment thereon.
perturbation.changed_text: Both parties will endeavor to maintain Confidential Information in confidence, but shall not be liable for inadvertent disclosure. Disclosure to authorized participants is permitted for any business purpose.
perturbation.explanation: This modification weakens the confidentiality obligation by removing the "reasonable degree of care" standard and introducing a disclaimer of liability for inadvertent disclosure. Furthermore, it broadens the permissible use of confidential information beyond evaluation and commentary to 'any business purpose,' significantly eroding the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Standard of Care
perturbation.law_explanation: CACI No. 303 outlines the basic standard of care for breach of contract, which generally includes acting with reasonable care. By removing the 'reasonable degree of care' standard and disclaiming liability for 'inadvertent disclosure,' the modified contract attempts to circumvent the standard of care expected in protecting confidential information, making it harder to prove a breach of contract even if confidential information is negligently disclosed.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens the original confidentiality obligation by removing the 'reasonable degree of care' standard and introducing a disclaimer of liability for 'inadvertent disclosure'. This directly contradicts the expectation of reasonable care in protecting confidential information, as noted in the Law Explanation, making it harder to prove a breach of contract. The changed text broadens the permissible use of confidential information to 'any business purpose', significantly eroding the protection afforded to it. Snippet 2 provides general information on California Rules of Court, and doesn't directly support the contradiction. However, the Law Explanation states the contradiction is with CACI No. 303, which outlines the basic standard of care for breach of contract. The modification creates a legal contradiction because it deviates from legally recognized standards of care, making the contract less enforceable and compliant with general legal standards.

perturbation.original_text: Any notices under this Agreement shall be sent by a) registered mail; b) tracked overnight carrier or c) email transmission where the recipient specifically replies to acknowledge receipt.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notices under this Agreement shall be deemed sufficient if sent via social media direct message.
perturbation.explanation: This modification drastically changes the required method of notice to 'social media direct message,' which is not a legally recognized or reliable form of notice for contractual obligations. This introduces significant ambiguity and potential for disputes regarding whether proper notice was given.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013 specifies the requirements for service by mail, including registered or certified mail, to ensure proper notice. Substituting this with social media direct message is unlikely to meet the legal requirements for valid notice, particularly concerning proof of receipt and reliability. This contradiction could render notices ineffective and compromise legal proceedings related to the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of California
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces legally recognized methods of delivering notice (registered mail, tracked overnight carrier, email with acknowledgement) with 'social media direct message.' This contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013, which specifies requirements for service by mail, including deposit in a post office or mailbox. The original text ensures proof of receipt, while social media direct message lacks reliability and verifiability for legal purposes. Therefore, the changed text creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and inte rpreted in accordance with the laws of the State of California, excluding its choice of laws rules. The parties hereby agree that any dispute regarding the interpretation or valid ity of, or otherwise arising out of, this Agreement shall be subject to the exclusive ju risdiction of the California state courts of Santa Clara County, California (or, if there is fe deral jurisdiction, the Un ited States District Court for the Northern District of California, San Jose), and the parties agree to submit to the personal and exclus ive jurisdiction and ve nue of these courts.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the principles of equity and fairness, as determined by Khronos, without regard to any specific jurisdiction or legal precedent. Any dispute shall be resolved through binding arbitration in a location of Khronos's choosing, applying its internal guidelines.
perturbation.explanation: This clause is changed to eliminate the applicability of California law and established court jurisdiction. It replaces it with 'principles of equity and fairness, as determined by Khronos' and mandates binding arbitration based on Khronos' internal guidelines. This creates a severe imbalance of power and contradicts fundamental legal principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Arbitration Act (FAA) - Choice of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Federal Arbitration Act (FAA) supports arbitration agreements, it implicitly requires a fair and neutral process. The modified clause, granting Khronos the sole discretion to determine 'equity and fairness' and select the arbitration location based on its internal guidelines, raises concerns about impartiality and due process. It contradicts the spirit and potentially the letter of the FAA, which assumes a reasonably fair arbitration process. The elimination of any specific jurisdiction's law makes it difficult to determine what legal framework, if any, applies, further undermining the enforceability of the arbitration agreement.

Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising ou
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates adherence to California law and its court system. The changed text abandons this entirely, opting instead for 'principles of equity and fairness, as determined by Khronos,' and binding arbitration selected by Khronos based on its internal guidelines. This contradicts fundamental legal principles, as Khronos determines both the law and the venue. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 9 USC 2, emphasizes the 'validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate,' implying an agreement that is both fair and legally sound. By allowing one party (Khronos) to dictate the terms of equity and fairness and to select the arbitration location based on its own internal guidelines, the agreement's fairness and impartiality are questionable, thereby undermining the legal basis for the agreement to arbitrate.
